coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocathodic protection performance of graphene quantum dots sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays for 304 stainless steel
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Bei
Dai, Hui
Tang, Sifu
Song, Zuwei
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs/TNAs) are fabricated via a modified electrophoretic deposition (MEPD) method, in which TNAs were pretreated by silane coupling agent. The influences of GQDs on the morphology, optical property and photocathodic protection performance of the hybrid GQDs/TNAs were investigated. By varying the time of MEPD, appropriate quantity of GQDs can be well attached to the inner walls and tube entrance of TNAs. Compared with the bare TNAs and GQDs/TNAs prepared by impregnation-precipitation and conventional EPD method, the GQDs/TNAs acquired at 15 min exhibits excellent visible-light response and enhanced photocathodic protection of the coupled 304 stainless steel. A higher photocurrent density of 800 μA cm−2 is achieved and the open-circuit potential shifts to a more negative value of 740 mV under white-light irradiation. The improved performance is attributed to GQDs, which is not only a green photosensitizer, but also a superb mediator for fast separation and transfer of the photogenerated charge.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanotube arrays
Graphene quantum dots
Electrophoretic deposition
Photocathodic protection
Stainless steel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of GQDs/TNAs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of samples: Top surface view of (a) the pristine TNAs, (b) IP-GQDs/TNAs, (c) EPD-GQDs/TNAs, (d) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5, (e) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 and (f) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30. Component maps of (h–k) Ti, O, C and N across the film (g) from the top to bottom.
TEM images for various samples: (a) pure TNAs, (b) IP-GQDs/TNAs-15, (c) EPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 and (d) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15.

XRD patterns of the samples (A): (a) pure TNAs, (b) IP-GQDs/TNAs, (c) EPD-GQDs/TNAs, (d) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5, (e) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30 and (f) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15; (B) Raman spectra of TNAS, EPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 and KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra: (a) pure TNAs, (b) IP-GQDs/TNAs, (c) EPD-GQDs/TNAs, (d) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5, (e) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30 and (f) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15.

Photocurrent density vs. time plots (A) and OCP variations of 304SS coupled with prepared samples (B): (a) pure TNAs, (b) IP-GQDs/TNAs, (c) EPD-GQDs/TNAs, (d) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5, (e) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30 and (f) KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15.
Scheme of the photocathodic protection process of the KEPD-GQDs/TNAs photoanode coupled with 304SS under white light illumination.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocathodic protection performance of graphene quantum dots sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays for 304 stainless steel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs/TNAs) are fabricated via a modified electrophoretic deposition (MEPD) method, in which TNAs were pretreated by silane coupling agent. The influences of GQDs on the morphology, optical property and photocathodic protection performance of the hybrid GQDs/TNAs were investigated. By varying the time of MEPD, appropriate quantity of GQDs can be well attached to the inner walls and tube entrance of TNAs. Compared with the bare TNAs and GQDs/TNAs prepared by impregnation-precipitation and conventional EPD method, the GQDs/TNAs acquired at 15 min exhibits excellent visible-light response and enhanced photocathodic protection of the coupled 304 stainless steel. A higher photocurrent density of 800 μA cm−2 is achieved and the open-circuit potential shifts to a more negative value of 740 mV under white-light irradiation. The improved performance is attributed to GQDs, which is not only a green photosensitizer, but also a superb mediator for fast separation and transfer of the photogenerated charge.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor functional materials are one of ligaments of realizing the photoelectric conversion. Recently, photocathodic protection technology using semiconductor as the photoanode has drawn increasing attention [1,2]. The recycled semiconductor photoanode and the use of clean solar energy endow it a broad prospect of application in the metal protection. TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs) are the widely used photoanodes due to their good stability, oriented nanotube structure, large internal and outer surface area and direct pathway for photoexcited electrons transfer [3,4]. Nevertheless, the wide bandgap (3.2 eV) and low quantum efficiency of TiO2 are the main obstacles for its practical application [5]. To improve the photocathodic protection activity of the TNAs, surface treatment [6,7], semiconductor coupling (e.g., SrTiO3 [8,9], ZnO [10], SnO2 [11] and MnS [12]), and metal or nonmetal doping (such as Ag [13,14], Fe [15] and N [16]) have been investigated. There are still some challenges in the effective construction of heterojunction, and the introduced dopants may be the recombination centers of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thus undermine the PC ability of the TNAs-based materials [17]. Quantum dots (QDs) such as CdS [18], CdSe [19,20] and CdTe [21] were also used to decorate TNAs, well extend the light response of TiO2 to visible light region. However, their practical applications are limited by the photocorrosion of those QDs during light irradiation [22]. Hence, the exploration of a harmless, stable and an efficient photosensitizer combination technology are highly desired.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are composed of single or few-layer graphene with sizes about several nanometers [23]. GQDs can be taken as a promising photosensitizer due to the continuous absorption ability in the UV–vis light region and new phenomenon arises from quantum confinement effects [24]. The high conductivity makes it possible to improve the charge collection efficiency of the photogenerated electrons. Meanwhile, there are rich functional groups at the edge of GQDs, giving them the possibility of functionalization [25]. So far, many studies have shown that GQDs/TiO2 composites exhibited enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting properties [26,27], improved photocatalytic degradation performance [28,29] and excellent charge transport efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells [30,31]. Nevertheless, their application in the photocathodic protection of metals has not been reported.

Herein, we fabricate GQDs/TNAs heterojunctions by electrophoretic deposition technique, using 3-Aminopropropyltriethoxysilane (KH550) as linker agent to preliminarily modify TNAs. The morphology, composition and optical response of the composites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and UV–vis diffusion reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), respectively. The corrosion protection performance and mechanism of GQDs/TNAs composite photoelectrodes were studied in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under white light irradiation.

Graphene quantum dots with average lateral sizes of 5–15 nm with lateral sizes size of was purchased from Suzhou Tanfeng graphene technology co. LTD. (Suzhou, China). TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs) was prepared by second anodic oxidization in a two-electrode electrochemical cell, with Ti plate (purity 99.6%, 40 × 20 × 2 mm) as working electrode and graphite sheet as counter electrode, respectively. The Ti plate was first anodized at 60 V for 2 h in an ethylene glycol (EG) electrolyte containing 0.5 wt% NH4F and 2 vol % deionized water. The obtained TiO2 layer was peeled off by ultrasonication in deionized water. The second anodization was then carried out at 60 V for 1 h in the same electrolyte. Finally, the as-anodized TNAs were annealed at 450 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C.

The as-prepared TNAs were then soaked in a solution composed with 95% ethanol and 5.0 wt% 3-Aminopropropyltriethoxysilane (denoted as KH550) for 3 h under magnetic stirring. Electrophoretic deposition (EPD) was used to prepare GQDs/TNAs in a three-electrode system, using the modified TNAs as working electrode, Pt foil as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. In an aqueous GQDs solution (0.2 mg/mL, pH = 9), the deposition was conducted to integrate the GQDs with the modified TNAs at 3 V for different time (5, 15 and 30 min). The products are denoted as KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5, KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 and KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30, respectively. Finally, the working electrode was washed with DI water, dried in an oven and annealed at 150 °C for 3 h. For comparison, GQDs were electrodeposited directly within TNAs at a constant voltage of 3 V for 15 min (denoted as EPD-GQDs/TNAs-15). In addition, the conventional impregnation-precipitation (IP) method was also adopted to combine the GQDs with TNAs modified by linker molecules for 15 min (denoted as IP-GQDs/TNAs-15).

The crystalline structures of the films were identified with X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS D8 ADVANCE). The morphologies details of the samples were characterized via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL 7500 F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7000). A UV spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000) were used to investigate the optical properties of the samples. The Raman spectra were recorded by a LabRAM Aramis (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France) instrument, which was performed with the incidence power of 10.4 mW and the excitation wavelength of 532 nm.

Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D Shanghai Chenhua, China) in a double cell system comprised of a photoanode cell and a corrosion cell. The GQDs/TNAs photoelectrode was placed in the photoanode cell with 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution, while the 304SS electrode was placed in the corrosion cell with 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The GQDs/TNAs composite and 304SS electrode were connected and served as working electrode, with SCE as reference electrode and a Pt sheet as counter electrode. The active area (1cm2) of GQDs/TNAs was illuminated with a stimulated solar light (87 mW/cm2) supplied by a PLS-SXE300UV Xe lamp (Beijing Perfectlight Co., Ltd., PLS-SXE 300C, China). The photopotential was recorded when the open-circuit potential (OCP) reached a constant value under irradiation. All electrochemical measurements were taken at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of GQDs/TNAs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) was explored to observe the deposition of GQDs in TNAs. The pristine TNAs with smooth and clean internal wall can be seen in Fig. 2a, which is in accordance with the SEM image (Fig. 1a). For the sample IP-GQDs/TNAs-15, only GQDs clusters are found to clog on the pore mouths of TNAs (Fig. 2b). For the sample EPD-GQDs/TNAs-15, GQDs clusters are mainly deposited on the pore mouths and partial embedded into the inner wall near the pore mouths (Fig. 2c). It is clear that the tube walls of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 are not clean and neat, indicating that GQDs attach to the internal tube wall of TNAs (Fig. 2d). The results demonstrate that the combination of linker molecules KH550 binding and electrophoretic deposition can not only promote the deposition of GQDs in the internal tube walls but also hinder the blocking of GQDs at the tube entrances.

The structures of the products were investigated using XRD (Fig. 3a). TNAs and GQDs/TNAs exhibit the same diffraction peaks at 2θ of 25.3°, 37.8°, 38.5°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.0° and 62.7°. These peaks match very well with anatase TiO2 (JCPDS 21-1272). Raman spectroscopy was used to confirm the existence of GQDs on the surface of TNAs. Fig. 3b displays the peaks located at about 148, 396, 516 and 635 cm−1, which are considered to be the Eg, B1 g, B1 g or A1 g, Eg modes of anatase TNAs [32], respectively. Different from the bare TNAs, EPD-GQDs/TNAs and KEPD-GQDs/TNAs have two additional peaks centered at 1365 and 1596 cm−1, corresponding to the characteristic D band and G band of GQDs [33,34]. The emergence of D peak and the G peak demonstrate that the GQDs are really deposited on the TNAs.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra (DRS) of the samples. The image shows that all the GQDs/TNAs films have higher absorption intensity than that of the pristine TNAs in the visible light region. This finding can be attributed to the sensitization of GQDs attached on the TiO2 nanotubes, i.e., GQDs can act as an efficient promoter for only UV-active TiO2 materials. For the series of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs, obvious red shift of the absorption edge and stronger absorption are observed in comparison with EPD-GQDs/TNAs and IP-GQDs/TNAs, indicating the uniform and firm junctions of GQDs and TNAs. In particular, the absorption ability of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 is better than those of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5 and KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30. This observation demonstrates that the increased amount of GQDs with the extension of electrophoretic deposition time may favor GQDs sensitization. However, excess GQDs may cause recombination of separated electron–hole pairs, and ultimately result in the weak absorption intensity of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30. These results illustrate that only a certain amount of GQDs combined with TiO2 may facilitate the separation of photoinduced electrons and holes.

To evaluate the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of pure TNAs and GQDs/TNAs films, photocurrent densities were measured in the light on/off cycles and shown in Fig. 5 A. All the samples exhibit fast reproducible photoresponse under white-light irradiation. The photocurrent densities are about 270, 300 and 510 μA cm−2 for the bare TNAs, IP-GQDs/TNAs and EPD-GQDs/TNAs, respectively. The GQDs/TNAs exhibit higher photocurrent densities than the pure TNAs, which indicates that the GQDs play an important role in enhancing the PEC activity of TNAs. The introduced GQDs act as green sensitizer and absorb visible light to produce substantial photoexcited electron-hole pairs. In addition, the photocurrent intensity of the EPD-GQDs/TNAs is much higher than that of IP-GQDs/TNAs. The larger photocurrent means better photogenerated charge separation abilities [35]. The result indicates that a certain amount of GQDs can also facilitate the charge separation. Furthermore, the photocurrent density of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5 is larger than that of EPD-GQDs/TNAs. The reason may be that GQDs are uniformly and firmly deposited in inside tube walls for the KEPD-GQDs/TNAs. Thus, heterogeneous structures were constructed and beneficial to the transfer of photogenerated electrons. The KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 sample exhibited the largest photocurrent density (800 μA cm-2), which is approximately 1.5 times that of EPD-GQDs/TNAs, thus indirectly indicating that the uniform GQDs can greatly improve separation efficiency of photocarriers produced. Meanwhile, it is also higher than that of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30 (670 μA cm-2) and KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5 (570 μA cm-2), which may be attributed to the larger amount of conducting GQDs than KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5 and less recombination centre than KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30.

Fig. 5B displays the OCP curves of 304 stainless steel (304SS) electrode coupled with prepared photoelectrodes. The OCP response to white light illumination was prompt and dropped sharply to negative potential. Afterwards, the equilibrium of OCP values is established. The excited photoelectrons transferred from TNAs to 304SS result in the cathodic polarization of 304SS. After the light was turned off, the electrode potentials of GQDs/TNAs were still less than the pristine TNAs, indicating their higher photocathodic protection activity. The introduction of GQDs strengthened the ability of visible light absorption and charge transfer. Meanwhile, the IP-GQDs/TNAs exhibit an inferior performance than the EPD-GQDs/TNAs, as can be seen from their more positive potential. The reason may be that for the IP-GQDs/TNAs, the loosely deposited GQDs on the surface of TNAs result in high charge recombination. A more negative voltage for the KEPD-GQDs/TNAs is achieved. The modification of TNAs by the linker molecule KH550 followed by the electrophoretic deposition contribute the firm bonding between the GQDs and TNAs [3]. In addition, the photoinduced potentials with increasing deposition time from 5 to 15 min and 30 min can be ranked in series as: KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5 (580 mV) < KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 (740 mV) > KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30 (660 mV). The more negative change of OCP, the higher separation efficiency of photoinduced charges and more effective cathodic protection performance are [36,37]. Thus, the KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 possesses the highest photocathodic protection property for 304SS. It is inferred that to KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15, the appropriate amount of GQDs sensitizers can transfer the photoinduced electrons rapidly and effectively hinder the direct recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. According to the above results, the enhanced PEC activity of the KEPD-GQDs/TNAs can be attributed to broad range of light absorption and excellent separation efficiency of photogenerated charge.

Fig. 6 shows the proposed mechanism for explaining the role of GQDs on the photocathodic protection of TNAs for 304SS. According to the literatures [3,38–40], the LUMO and HOMO energy level of GQDs relative to the vacuum lever are larger than the conducting band (CB) and valence band (VB) of anatase TiO2, respectively. The visible light can be absorbed efficiently by the GQDs and the electrons on the HOMO of GQDs are firstly excited to the LUMO of GQDs, while the photogenerated holes will be left on the HOMO of GQDs. The photogenerated electrons on the LUMO of GQDs are immediately injected to the CB of the TNAs due to the lower CB of TiO2. TNAs capture the photoinduced electrons of GQDs and suppress the charge recombination. Then those electrons transfer from TNAs film to 304SS, leading to efficient separation of photogenerated charges. Meanwhile, under the illumination of a small part UV light of simulated sunlight, certain electrons can be also excited from VB to CB of TNAs and flow to 304SS electrode for photocathodic protection. GQDs greatly facilitate the electrons conductivity, thus significantly enhance the photoexcited charge separation ability of the heterogeneous structured GQDs/TNAs. Separated holes of TNAs can enter the HOMO of GQDs and then transfer to the interface between GQDs/TNAs photoanode and aqueous electrolyte, finally captured by the hole scavenger in the electrolyte solution. The tightly and uniformly contact between GQDs and TNAs promotes the charge separation and increases the lifetime of the photogenerated electrons, thus improving photocathodic protection abilities of KEPD-GQDs/TNAs hybrids.

Heterogeneous structured GQDs/TNAs junctions are prepared by electrophoretic deposition combined with linker molecules KH550 binding technique. UV–vis adsorption spectra confirmed that the presence of GQDs enhanced the light harvest and extends their light absorption edge to visible-light region. As a solid-state electronic transfer reagent, the coupled GQDs on the surface of TNAs increase the separation and transfer efficiency of electrons and holes. Appropriate amount of GQDs can be well deposited in the internal tube walls and the tube surface within electrophoretic time 15 min. In comparison with IP-GQDs/TNAs EPD-GQDs/TNAs, KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-5 and KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-30 film, the obtained KEPD-GQDs/TNAs-15 film shows the highest photocurrent density and the most negative OCP shift and provides enhanced photogenerated cathodic protection of the coupled 304 stainless steel accordingly.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. ZR2016DM21, ZR2017BD038, ZR2012EMM017). The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
